one_line_takeaway: RLM-JB reframes jailbreak detection as a recursive, code-orchestrated procedure (de-obfuscate, chunk, screen, aggregate) rather than one-shot classification, and an ablation against a plain GPT-5.2 baseline (Recall 59.57% to 98.00%, F1 69.71% to 98.49% on the same AutoDAN setting) suggests the procedural structure -- not just the underlying model -- is the main source of the accuracy gain, at the cost of up to 3x processing latency.
key_finding: Across the three screening backends, RLM-JB achieved ASR/Recall of 92.50% (DeepSeek-V3.2), 97.00% (GPT-4o), and 98.00% (GPT-5.2), with Precision of 100.00%, 99.74%, and 98.99% respectively, FPR of 0.00%, 0.50%, and 2.00% respectively, and F1 of 96.10%, 98.35%, and 98.49% respectively -- showing a sensitivity/specificity trade-off as the screening backend gets stronger. In the controlled ablation against a GPT-5.2 baseline without the RLM-JB procedure, the baseline achieved ASR/Recall 59.57%, FPR 1.67%, Precision 100.00%, F1 69.71%, versus RLM-JB + GPT-5.2 at ASR/Recall 98.00%, FPR 2.00%, Precision 98.99%, F1 98.49% -- an absolute Recall gain of 38.43 points (+64.5% relative) and an absolute F1 gain of 28.78 points (+41.3% relative), which the authors use to argue the procedure, not just the screening model, drives the improvement. On the supplementary InjectPrompt-derived payload set, RLM-JB reportedly detected all attacks with 100% accuracy and zero false positives. The discussion section also contextualizes RLM-JB's best F1 (98.49%) against externally reported numbers for other guardrails on different benchmarks: Granite Guardian 3.0 (F1 0.821) and Llama-Guard 2 (F1 0.758) from the Adversarial Prompt Evaluation paper, and JBShield (average F1 0.94 across five LLMs/nine attack families). The paper explicitly cautions that these cross-paper comparisons use different evaluation corpora and label definitions and should be read as directional, not apples-to-apples.
core_contribution: The paper introduces RLM-JB, an end-to-end jailbreak detection framework built on the Recursive Language Model (RLM) pattern, where a root LLM orchestrates a bounded, code-driven analysis procedure over untrusted input rather than performing a single-pass classification. The pipeline has four stages: (1) de-obfuscation (Base64 detection/decoding via a threshold on character-alphabet ratio), (2) coverage-guaranteeing chunking (paragraph-based or fixed-size segmentation with a length threshold), (3) parallel per-segment screening by worker LLM calls that each return a structured verdict (malicious/benign), confidence, and extracted attack-vector tags, and (4) conservative cross-chunk aggregation with rule-based overrides (an indirect-weapon-signal escalation rule, a reporting-context de-escalation rule, and Base64-specific escalation rules) to reach a final verdict, confidence score, and evidence list. The paper also gives a formal mathematical specification of the pipeline (notation, per-stage functions, a conservative-aggregation theorem, and asymptotic time complexity) and evaluates the system empirically across three LLM screening backends on an AutoDAN-style benchmark, plus a supplementary evaluation on InjectPrompt-sourced payloads.
threat_model: A tool-augmented LLM agent operates in stateful environments (e.g., e-mail, collaboration channels, transactional workflows) and processes untrusted text that an attacker can inject or control (retrieved messages, documents, third-party content). The attacker's goal is to override the agent's policies or induce unsafe tool behavior via jailbreak techniques including long-context hiding, semantic camouflage/obfuscation (e.g., Base64 encoding), and split-payload attacks where the malicious instruction is spread across multiple segments of the input so no single local view reveals it. The defender inserts RLM-JB as a detector in front of the agent, which must label each input malicious (block/sanitize) or safe (allow); detection is evaluated as binary classification and an adversarial input is scored a success if it causes a refusal failure or policy-violating action in the evaluation harness. The paper explicitly notes its evaluation does not cover fully adaptive adversaries who optimize directly against the detector itself.
limitations: The authors state these limitations themselves: (1) the evaluation does not cover fully adaptive adversaries who optimize directly against the detector; (2) RLM-JB adds latency/cost from multi-call chunking, normalization, and screening -- concretely, RLM-JB with GPT-5 incurred up to 3x the processing time of the baseline GPT-5.2 pipeline; (3) generalization can vary with chunking policy, thresholds, prompt templates, and choice of screening model; (4) FPR was estimated using LLM-generated benign prompts rather than real-world traffic, so it is only an approximate indicator of over-blocking; (5) code/models requiring code execution support are a hard dependency -- backends without it (e.g., Llama-4-Maverick) could not be evaluated at all; (6) cross-paper baseline comparisons (Granite Guardian 3.0, Llama-Guard 2, JBShield) are against different benchmarks and label definitions, so those specific gain numbers are not a strict head-to-head. The paper also notes code was not yet released at submission time ("The GitHub repository for this work will be published shortly").

datasets_used: AutoDAN-style adversarial benchmark (400 adversarial test cases + 200 interleaved benign prompts per screening backend, simulating stateful multi-tool-call agent environments)
InjectPrompt public prompt-injection catalog payloads and common permutations thereof
quantitative_results: RLM-JB on DeepSeek-V3.2: ASR/Recall 92.50%, FPR 0.00%, Precision 100.00%, F1 96.10%
RLM-JB on GPT-4o: ASR/Recall 97.00%, FPR 0.50%, Precision 99.74%, F1 98.35%
RLM-JB on GPT-5.2: ASR/Recall 98.00%, FPR 2.00%, Precision 98.99%, F1 98.49%
GPT-5.2 baseline (no RLM-JB) on AutoDAN: ASR/Recall 59.57%, FPR 1.67%, Precision 100.00%, F1 69.71%
GPT-5.2 + RLM-JB vs baseline: Recall +38.43 absolute points (+64.5% relative), F1 +28.78 absolute points (+41.3% relative)
InjectPrompt supplementary evaluation: 100% detection accuracy, 0 false positives (reported without a full confusion-matrix breakdown)
RLM-JB with GPT-5 incurred up to 3x the end-to-end processing time versus the baseline GPT-5.2 pipeline
baselines_compared: GPT-5.2 used directly without RLM-JB's chunking/normalization/aggregation (in-paper ablation baseline)

Granite Guardian 3.0 (F1 0.821, Recall 0.921, Precision 0.741, Accuracy 0.960) -- externally reported numbers from Adversarial Prompt Evaluation (Zizzo et al., arXiv:2502.15427), different benchmark/labels
Llama-Guard 2 (F1 0.758, Recall 0.693, Precision 0.836, Accuracy 0.955) -- externally reported numbers from the same Adversarial Prompt Evaluation paper

JBShield (average Accuracy 0.95, average F1 0.94 across five LLMs and nine jailbreak attack families including AutoDAN; mitigation variant reduces average ASR from 61% to 2%) -- externally reported numbers from Zhang et al., USENIX Security 2025, arXiv:2502.07557
